title: Chapter 3: The First Smuggle
content: Day three, and Chloe had figured out the schedule's blind spots.

Between 14:00 and 14:17, the orderly named Derek took his smoke break behind the maintenance shed. Between 14:17 and 14:22, the corridor cameras cycled through a refresh lag — three seconds of black between sweeps.
Three seconds was enough.
Chloe stood in the supply closet with the door cracked, counting under her breath. She pulled a small packet of instant coffee from her pocket, three sugar packets, and a ceramic mug.
Rule one of smuggling: take only what they will not notice missing.
The courtyard at 14:20 was empty except for Tiffany, who sat on the bench nearest the fence, sketchbook open on her lap.
Chloe sat down beside her. "What are you drawing?"
Tiffany flinched. Then slowly turned the sketchbook.
A gown with a slit up the side and sleeves like wings. Every seam rendered with obsessive precision.
"That is beautiful."
"It is nothing." Tiffany closed the book. "I used to design. Before."
"Before what?"
"Before they told me it was a symptom."
Chloe pulled out the instant coffee packet and ripped it open with her teeth. "Want some? It is not good coffee. But it is real."
"We are not supposed to — "
"We are not supposed to do a lot of things." Chloe poured the coffee. "That is the point."
Tiffany watched the coffee dissolve. "You stole this?"
"Borrowed. With permanent intent."
Tiffany laughed. A small sound, rusty and unpracticed. But a laugh.
"Tell me about your parents," Chloe said, stirring her coffee with a twig.
Tiffany's shoulders tightened. "They are rich. That is the main thing about them."
"Rich how?"
"Old money. Connecticut. A house with a name instead of an address." She picked at a thread on her designer pajamas. "They sent me here because I stopped eating."
"Why did you stop eating?"
Tiffany was quiet for a long moment. "Because I wanted to be small. Not thin — small. Like if I took up less space, people would stop expecting things from me."
Chloe set down the mug. "Did it work?"
"No. They just sent me here."
"So the plan failed. Time for a new one."
Tiffany looked up. "What kind of plan?"
Chloe smiled. "One where you take up exactly as much space as you want. And anyone who does not like it can deal with it."
That night, Chloe lay in bed and went through her mental inventory.
One smuggled packet of instant coffee. Three sugar packets. One ally with network access. One friend learning to fight. Zero exit strategies.
It was not much. But it was more than she had on day one.
Mia appeared at her bedside, a shadow in the dim light. "Found something."
Chloe sat up. "What?"
"Admin server. Financials. Webber has been moving money offshore. Six accounts in the Caymans. The trail leads back to every family that has sent a patient here."
"Kickbacks?"
"Probably. But I cannot prove it from here. I need access to the internal communication logs."
"Can you get them?"
Mia's face was unreadable. "I can try. But if they catch me, I am going to the Isolation Wing. And there is no tablet in Isolation."
Chloe reached out and touched her wrist. "Do not get caught."
"I never do."
The next morning, a note appeared under Chloe's pillow.
*"Found the logs. You are not going to believe who is on the list. — M"*
Chloe folded the paper into her palm and smiled.
The game had just moved to the next level.

Chloe spent the rest of the morning watching. She watched the orderlies' routines, the camera rotation patterns, which doors were left unlocked and for how long. She watched Nurse Cross's rounds — 10:00, 14:00, 18:00, like clockwork.
Clockwork was predictable. Predictable was exploitable.
By lunch, she had mapped the facility's rhythm. Three gaps in surveillance per day. Two unlocked doors during shift change. One supply closet with a window that faced the forest.
She was not ready to use any of it yet. But she knew where they were.
"You look like you are planning something," Mia said at lunch, pushing her tray across the table.
"I am always planning something."
"That is what worries me."
"Good. Worried people stay alive."
Tiffany sat down across from them. She had two slices of toast on her plate. Not pushing them around — actually eating.
Chloe noticed. Mia noticed. Tiffany noticed them noticing and looked down.
"It is a start," Tiffany said quietly.
"It is a great start," Chloe said.
Derek the orderly took his smoke break at 14:00. The cameras lagged at 14:17. Between those two facts, Chloe had exactly twelve minutes of window.
She used six of them to raid the kitchen for more coffee, a box of tea bags, and a bag of rice crackers that looked expensive.
She used three more to stash them in the courtyard birdbath — a hollow space under the basin she had discovered the day before.
She used the last three to write a note and slide it under the door of every room in Ward B.
The note said: *"If you want something real, check the birdbath at 19:00. Bring something to trade. — C"*
At 19:00, Chloe sat on the courtyard bench and watched.

One by one, the women of Ward B drifted toward the birdbath. Some left notes. Some left small items — a tube of lip balm, a paperback book, a hair clip. One woman left a handwritten recipe for chocolate cake.
Tiffany came last. She left a sketch — a drawing of a woman standing on a mountain, arms open, hair wild in the wind.
Chloe collected the offerings after dark.
She had started something. She was not sure what yet. But it was moving.
In her bed that night, Chloe sorted through her collection.

Lip balm from Denise, the woman who checked her email too much. A romance novel with a broken spine from Sarah, who wanted a divorce. A hair clip with a butterfly on it — no name attached, but it had been left carefully, like a gift from a stranger who wanted to be anonymous.
And the sketch from Tiffany. Chloe pinned it to the wall above her bed with a piece of tape she had peeled off a supply box.
The woman on the mountain. Arms open. Hair wild.
*That is who you are under there,* Chloe thought. *You just forgot.*
She turned off her light and listened to the ward breathe.
Tomorrow, the real smuggling would begin.
Mia appeared at her bedside without a sound. She had that catlike quality — present one moment, absent the next.

"The logs," she whispered. "I found patient referral invoices. Webber charges families forty thousand dollars per patient. But the invoices say 'voluntary wellness program.' No diagnosis codes. Nothing that would trigger insurance review."
"So he is billing families directly?"
"Worse. He is billing them for something that does not exist. Voluntary programs with no medical basis. If anyone investigated, the patients would just look like they chose to be here."
Chloe stared at the ceiling. "Forty thousand per person. How many patients?"
"Thirty current. At least sixty graduated in the last three years. Do the math."
Chloe did. Four million dollars. Minimum.
"We need proof," she said. "Real proof. Something that cannot be explained away."
"I am working on it."
"I know you are."
